problem_statement: As AI coding agents increasingly edit shared codebases, organizations need a way to know where AI interventions are lower-risk versus where they require additional human oversight; the paper investigates whether code quality (CodeHealth) predicts how reliably LLMs can safely refactor code.
magnitude_evidence: About 80% of developers already use AI tools (Stack Overflow 2025); Gartner predicts growth from 14% in early 2024 to 90% by 2028; less than 10% of organizations methodically track technical debt; developers waste up to 42% of their time due to poor code quality (Stripe 2018)
insufficiency_evidence: Medium-sized LLMs break test suites at high rates on Unhealthy code (e.g., Gemma passes only 40.58% Unhealthy); Claude's statements about 'zero functionality changes' are overconfident – 'sometimes the agent indeed breaks behavior'; PPL fails as a file-level AI-friendliness metric (no meaningful association with CH)
solution_mechanisms: CodeHealth as risk-routing signal for AI deployment
high CH as soft gate for AI deployment
focus early AI on Healthy code
raise CH before scaling LLM refactoring
expect more human oversight on low-CH code
decision-tree/logistic-regression prediction of break rate from CH
code-quality agent or CH tool in agent toolbox
